old_claim.note: Military Field Medicine provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Herbal Medicine Preparation supplies the plant-remedy and medicine-making practice explicitly represented in monastic infirmary care; military field medicine is not required by this monastic ward scope.

source_shape.source_locator: NLM says the Bury St Edmunds medical manuscript is largely dedicated to medical diagnosis and herbal remedies, with recipe collections and instructions for cures and herbs used in monastic healing.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Monastic infirmary care is supported by monastic manuscript and herbal-remedy practice, not military field care.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source ties monastic medical care to infirmaries, pharmacies, manuscripts, diagnosis, and herbal remedies; it provides no requirement for battlefield medicine.

ontology_before: Monastic Infirmaries depended on Military Field Medicine, implying a battlefield-care lineage for religious medical wards.
ontology_after: Monastic Infirmaries depends on Monastic Scriptoria and Herbal Medicine Preparation, matching the source-backed medical manuscript and remedy-preparation scope.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: monastic_infirmaries now depends on herbal_medicine_preparation and no longer directly depends on military_field_medicine.
would_reject_if: The node is rescoped to battlefield monastic medical services rather than monastery infirmary wards.
A source shows monastic infirmaries directly derived from military field hospitals rather than monastic care and remedy preparation.
A dedicated monastic_hospital_wards node is added and needs a different institutional prerequisite.
short_reason: Monastic infirmaries are herbal and monastic-care institutions, not battlefield medicine.
